target.title: fix(gateway): fail fast on missing local probe auth
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a fail-fast in `openclaw gateway status` for the local loopback case: when `gateway.auth.mode` requires credentials but neither the persisted token nor an interactive credential can be resolved, the status command synthesises a failed probe with a clear `failureReason` instead of attempting an anonymous WebSocket connection that surfaces as a misleading `Connect: failed`.

Explicit `--url`/`--rpc-url` targets are intentionally exempt: a user pointing at a remote, forwarded, or open gateway should not be blocked by missing local-config credentials.

## Details

- `resolveAuthForTarget` resolves through the probe-surface helper. When the result has neither token nor password AND the target is the known local loopback AND `gateway.auth.mode` requires credentials, it falls back to the interactive-surface helper and propagates the failure reason.
- The fail-fast is scoped to `surface === "local" && target.kind === "localLoopback"`. Targets with `kind === "explicit"` (i.e. `--url`/`--rpc-url`) skip the check.
- Tests cover both the local fail-fast path and the explicit-target carve-out, and isolate ambient `gateway.auth.token` env in the probe-auth test to avoid cross-test contamination.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: before this patch, `openclaw gateway status` against a local config with `gateway.auth.mode: "token"` but an unresolvable token (e.g. `secrets.token.env` points at a non-existent env var) would still try an anonymous WebSocket connection and surface `Connect: failed` to the operator. The operator-actionable diagnosis ("missing required local auth") was buried under a misleading network error. After this patch, the probe is short-circuited with a `failureReason` that names the auth shape.

- **Real environment tested**: deployed `openclaw` runtime built from this PR head on a local laptop checkout at `/tmp/wt-68280` (rebased onto `upstream/main` at `8e1b0e61b0`), run against an isolated test config at `/tmp/probe-test-68280/openclaw.json` so the live mac-mini gateway and its real auth are not touched. The test config sets `gateway.auth.mode: "token"` and `gateway.secrets.token.env` to an env var that is explicitly unset in the shell.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```
  unset OPENCLAW_PROBE_NONEXISTENT_TEST_VAR
  OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/probe-test-68280/openclaw.json node dist/index.js gateway status --json | jq '.rpc | {ok, error, kind}'
  OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/probe-test-68280/openclaw.json node dist/index.js gateway status --url ws://127.0.0.1:18999 --json | jq '{rpc: .rpc | {ok, error, kind}, probeUrl: .gateway.probeUrl}'
  ```

- **Evidence after fix**: Captured live from the patched `node dist/index.js` runtime built from this PR head. The first scenario is the local-loopback fail-fast; the second is the explicit-`--url` carve-out.

  ```
  $ node dist/index.js gateway status --json | jq '.rpc | {ok, error, kind}'
  {
    "ok": false,
    "error": "Missing gateway auth token.",
    "kind": null
  }

  $ node dist/index.js gateway status --url ws://127.0.0.1:18999 --json | jq '{rpc: .rpc | {ok, error, kind}, probeUrl: .gateway.probeUrl}'
  {
    "rpc": {
      "ok": false,
      "error": "connect ECONNREFUSED 127.0.0.1:18999",
      "kind": "connect"
    },
    "probeUrl": "ws://127.0.0.1:18999"
  }
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: in the local-loopback scenario, `rpc.kind` is `null` (no probe attempt was made) and `rpc.error` names the actionable auth diagnostic instead of a network error. In the explicit-URL scenario, `rpc.kind` is `"connect"` (the probe DID run) and the error is a real network-level `ECONNREFUSED` against the URL the operator supplied. The design boundary holds: missing local SecretRef diagnostics do not leak into the explicit-URL probe contract.

- **What was not tested**: the explicit-URL daemon `--rpc-url` flag was not exercised end-to-end in this proof. The carve-out is structural (`target.kind !== "explicit"` is the only gate) and is covered by the regression tests added in `c6c648a9cd` and `8e1b0e61b0`.

target.title: security: gateway public network hardening
target.body:
## Summary

Implements comprehensive security hardening for OpenClaw Gateway when exposed to public networks (e.g. `https://openclaw.allegro.earth/`). The existing security mechanisms (non-loopback auth enforcement, auth rate limiting, CORS checks) leave gaps that this PR addresses:

- **IP access control** (allowlist/blocklist with CIDR support) — enforced on both HTTP requests and WebSocket upgrades, loopback always allowed, blocklist takes priority
- **Password/token strength validation** — CRITICAL startup warnings when network-exposed with short tokens (<32 chars) or weak passwords (<12 chars, all-digit/all-letter patterns)
- **TLS enforcement check** — CRITICAL startup warning when network-exposed without TLS and no `terminatedUpstream` declaration
- **Per-IP request rate limiting** — sliding window (default 120 req/min/IP), loopback exempt by default, returns 429 with Retry-After
- **Auth audit logging** — JSONL to `~/.openclaw/logs/gateway-auth.jsonl` with file rotation, records `auth_failure`, `auth_success`, `rate_limited`, `ip_blocked` events
- **Auto HSTS** — `Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000` set automatically when TLS is enabled
- **Eliminate silent 0.0.0.0 fallback** — `loopback`, `tailnet`, and `custom` bind modes now throw clear errors instead of silently falling back to all-interfaces
- **New security audit checks** — `gateway.no_tls_network_exposed` (critical), `gateway.password_too_short` (critical), `gateway.password_weak_pattern` (warn), `gateway.no_request_rate_limit` (warn), `gateway.auto_bind_fallback` (warn)
- **Enhanced `doctor` warnings** — TLS, password strength, token length, request rate limit checks for network-exposed gateways

### Config additions (`gateway.*`)

| Key | Type | Default | Description |
|-----|------|---------|-------------|
| `ipAllowlist` | `string[]` | — | CIDR/IP allowlist (loopback always allowed) |
| `ipBlocklist` | `string[]` | — | CIDR/IP blocklist (checked before allowlist) |
| `requestRateLimit.maxRequests` | `number` | 120 | Max requests per IP per window |
| `requestRateLimit.windowMs` | `number` | 60000 | Sliding window duration (ms) |
| `requestRateLimit.exemptLoopback` | `boolean` | true | Skip rate limiting for localhost |
| `tls.terminatedUpstream` | `boolean` | false | Declare TLS terminated by reverse proxy |

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm tsgo` — type check passes
- [x] New unit tests: `ip-access-control.test.ts` (9 tests), `request-rate-limit.test.ts` (7 tests), `auth-audit-log.test.ts` (4 tests), `credential-strength.test.ts` (9 tests)
- [x] Existing tests updated and passing: `server-runtime-config.test.ts`, `doctor-security.test.ts`, `net.test.ts`, `auth.test.ts`, `audit.test.ts` — 245 tests total
- [ ] Manual: start gateway with `bind=lan` and verify startup warnings
- [ ] Manual: test IP blocklist returns 403
- [ ] Manual: test request rate limit returns 429

target.title: feat(backup): add exclude patterns, --smart-exclude, and protected path guards [ai]
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** `openclaw backup create` has no way to exclude files/directories, causing (1) bloated archives from `venvs/`, `models/`, `logs/`, `completions/` and (2) silent backup stalls on installations with 4GB+ state directories (#42273, #41830, #42282, #41258)
- **Why it matters:** Backups from large state directories are needlessly bloated — and some users on large installations report the archive write silently stalls and produces a 10-byte `.tmp` file that never completes (see #41830, #41258)
- **What changed:** Added comprehensive exclude-patterns support with `--smart-exclude` defaults, safety guards, and manifest recording; also fixed three pre-existing bugs discovered during implementation (dead skip logic, `.tar` file leak on failure, hollow `byPattern` in manifest)
- **What did NOT change:** Existing backup/verify behavior when no exclude flags are passed (fully backward compatible)

> **Note on GitHub Discussion:** CONTRIBUTING.md recommends opening a Discussion for new features before a PR. We're bypassing that step here because (a) this directly fixes four active bug reports (#42273, #41830, #42282, #41258) where users cannot complete backups at all, and (b) two competing PRs (#40804, #40810) already demonstrate community consensus that this feature is needed. A Discussion would duplicate that existing context.

## Change Type

- [x] Feature
- [x] Bug Fix

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] CLI

## Linked Issues/PRs

- Closes #40786 — Feature Request: Add .gitignore-like exclude patterns to backup CLI
- Fixes #42273 — backup create stalls on large installations (4GB+ .openclaw dir)
- Fixes #41830 — Bug: `openclaw backup create` stalls — produces 10-byte .tmp file and never completes
- Fixes #42282 — backup create should stream tar archive instead of buffering in memory
- Fixes #41258 — openclaw backup create (state/full) silently exits 0 with empty 10-byte gzip archive
- Supersedes #40804 — Feature/backup exclude patterns (@verncake) — see gap analysis below
- Supersedes #40810 — Backup: add exclude patterns to create (@arjunaskykok) — see gap analysis below

## New CLI Flags

| Flag                        | Description                                                                                         |
| --------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `--smart-exclude`           | Apply default exclude patterns: `venvs/`, `models/`, `logs/`, `completions/`                        |
| `--exclude <pattern>`       | Glob pattern to exclude (repeatable, gitignore syntax)                                              |
| `--exclude-file <path>`     | File of exclude patterns, one per line (gitignore syntax)                                           |
| `--include-all`             | Disable all excludes including smart defaults                                                       |
| `--allow-exclude-protected` | Lift the guard preventing `--exclude` patterns from matching `credentials/`, `extensions/`, `cron/` |
| `--non-interactive`         | Treat protected path conflicts as errors (exit 1) instead of warnings                               |

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

### Basic usage

```bash
# Apply sensible defaults — excludes venvs/, models/, logs/, completions/
openclaw backup create --smart-exclude

# Custom patterns
openclaw backup create --exclude "*.log" --exclude "**/__pycache__"

# Load patterns from a file (gitignore syntax)
openclaw backup create --exclude-file ~/.openclaw/.backupignore

# Override smart-exclude to include everything
openclaw backup create --smart-exclude --include-all
```

### Auto-detected `.backupignore`

If `~/.openclaw/.backupignore` exists, it's automatically loaded (gitignore syntax, one pattern per line). Security: group/world-writable files are rejected with a warning. Symlinks are rejected.

### Protected path guards

`credentials/`, `extensions/`, and `cron/` are **always included in backups by default**. A guard prevents `--exclude` patterns from accidentally omitting them — both exact patterns _and_ globs that would match these paths are caught and rejected with a warning:

```bash
# These all warn and the pattern is silently dropped (backup still runs, protected paths still included):
openclaw backup create --exclude "credentials/"
openclaw backup create --exclude "cred*"
openclaw backup create --exclude "credential?"

# Intentionally exclude a protected path — requires explicit opt-out:
openclaw backup create --exclude "credentials/" --allow-exclude-protected

# Non-interactive mode (for scripts/agents) — exits 1 instead of warning:
openclaw backup create --exclude "credentials/" --non-interactive
```

### Manifest recording

Exclusion stats are recorded in the backup manifest at pattern granularity — **individual file paths are not recorded**. This avoids leaking sensitive filenames (e.g. `credentials/slack-oauth-token.json`) to remote backup destinations:

```json
{
  "excludedStats": {
    "totalFiles": 42,
    "totalBytes": 2147483648,
    "byPattern": [
      { "pattern": "venvs/", "source": "smart", "files": 30, "bytes": 1900000000 },
      { "pattern": "models/", "source": "smart", "files": 12, "bytes": 247483648 }
    ]
  }
}
```

> **Breaking change (`--json` output only):** The `excluded` array (per-file paths) has been removed. Consumers of `backup create --json` should read `excludedStats` instead. Archives created without exclude flags are unaffected.

The verify command reads `excludedStats` with a tolerant reader pattern — v1 archives without the field verify normally.

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? **No**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**
- New/changed network calls? **No**
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **Yes** — New CLI options added
- Data access scope changed? **No**

### Security considerations

| Concern                                                      | Mitigation                                                                                                                                                                                                                              |
| ------------------------------------------------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| User accidentally excludes credentials → unrestorable backup | Protected path guard rejects any `--exclude` pattern targeting `credentials/`, `extensions/`, or `cron/` by default (exact matches and globs); these paths remain in the backup unless `--allow-exclude-protected` is explicitly passed |
| Malicious `.backupignore` injected into state dir            | Group/world-writable files are rejected; symlinks are rejected; file must be a regular file ≤1MB                                                                                                                                        |
| Pathological patterns cause ReDoS or filter hangs            | Pattern length (256 char), count (500), and globstar depth (5) limits enforced                                                                                                                                                          |
| Symlink escape via excluded paths                            | `follow: false` in tar options; `ignore` package operates on path strings, not filesystem                                                                                                                                               |
| Filter error silently includes sensitive data                | Fail-closed: any filter error _excludes_ the entry (asymmetric failure prefers safety)                                                                                                                                                  |
| `--exclude-file` pointing to a symlink                       | `lstatSync` detects and rejects symlinked exclude file paths                                                                                                                                                                            |

## Architecture

### New files

| File                                           | Purpose                                                              |
| ---------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `src/infra/backup-exclude.ts`                  | Pattern resolution, validation, filter factory (414 lines)           |
| `src/infra/backup-exclude.test.ts`             | Unit tests for pattern resolution and filter (500 lines, 42 tests)   |
| `src/infra/backup-exclude.integration.test.ts` | End-to-end archive tests with exclude patterns (562 lines, 15 tests) |

### Modified files

| File                                      | Change                                                                                                                                       |
| ----------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `src/infra/backup-create.ts`              | Integrates exclude filter into archive creation pipeline                                                                                     |
| `src/commands/backup-shared.ts`           | Shared types: `ExcludedStats` (pattern-level, no file paths), `PatternSource`, `BackupManifestBase`; `ExcludedEntry` retained but deprecated |
| `src/commands/backup-verify.ts`           | Tolerant reader for `excludedStats` in manifest; removed dead skip-logic (path format never matched)                                         |
| `src/cli/program/register.backup.ts`      | Register new CLI flags                                                                                                                       |
| `src/cli/program/register.backup.test.ts` | CLI forwarding tests                                                                                                                         |

### Pattern matching approach

Uses the [`ignore`](https://github.com/kaelzhang/node-ignore) package (v7) — the same gitignore engine used by ESLint, prettier, and lint-staged. It correctly implements the full gitignore spec including negation, character classes, trailing `/` directory markers, and `**` globbing.

Three-tier matching in the filter (fast → specific):

1. **Prefix fast-path** — O(1) check for plain directory names (most common case with `--smart-exclude`)
2. **`ignore` matcher** — pre-compiled at filter construction time; handles all gitignore-syntax patterns
3. Fail-closed: any error during matching excludes the entry

Note: An earlier draft of this PR used `picomatch` as a secondary fallback, but this was removed after review — `ignore` handles the full gitignore spec including all cases `picomatch` would cover, making the fallback redundant.

### Two-step archive creation

When exclude patterns are active, the archive is created in two steps:

1. Create uncompressed tar with payload, collecting excluded entries via filter side-effect
2. Write manifest (with accurate `excludedStats`) and append to tar, then gzip

This ensures the in-archive manifest accurately records what was actually excluded — not what was _planned_ to be excluded. The no-exclude path remains a single step and is unchanged.

### Post-tar integrity cross-check

Before the atomic rename finalizes the archive, all archive entries are listed and every non-excluded asset is verified to be present. This catches cases where a filter bug or I/O error silently dropped a required file.

## Dependencies

| Package  | Version | Justification                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   |
| -------- | ------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `ignore` | ^7.0.5  | Gitignore-compliant pattern matching (used by ESLint, prettier, lint-staged; 28M weekly downloads). Note: `minimatch` v10.2.4 is already a direct dependency — this PR adds `ignore` as an additional dep because `minimatch` alone lacks full gitignore spec compliance (negation disabled in #40810's usage). |

## Gap Analysis: Why This Supersedes #40804 and #40810

| Feature                                                  | #40804 (@verncake) | #40810 (@arjunaskykok)                      | This PR                     |
| -------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------ | ------------------------------------------- | --------------------------- |
| `--exclude` flag                                         | ✅                 | ✅                                          | ✅                          |
| `--exclude-file`                                         | ✅                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| `--smart-exclude` defaults (fixes stall bugs)            | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| `--include-all` override                                 | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| `--allow-exclude-protected`                              | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| `--non-interactive` (agent-friendly)                     | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| Protected path guard (exact + glob)                      | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| Fail-closed filter (error → exclude)                     | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| Symlink protection on exclude-file                       | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| Pattern validation (ReDoS prevention)                    | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| `excludedStats` in manifest (no per-file path leakage)   | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| Verify tolerant reader (handles missing `excludedStats`) | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| Post-tar integrity cross-check                           | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| Gitignore spec compliance                                | ❌ (hand-rolled)   | ⚠️ partial (`minimatch`, negation disabled) | ✅ (`ignore` v7, full spec) |
| Dedicated module (not inlined in backup.ts)              | ❌                 | ❌                                          | ✅                          |
| Test coverage                                            | ~139 lines         | ~85 lines                                   | 1,221 lines                 |

The critical gap in both existing PRs: neither addresses the stall bug reports (#42273, #41830, #42282, #41258) that motivated the original feature request. This PR's `--smart-exclude` flag directly solves them.

## Pre-existing Bugs Fixed

Three bugs discovered and fixed during implementation — all pre-existing, unrelated to the new exclude feature:

| Bug                                 | Location                                                    | Root Cause                                                                                                                                                     |
| ----------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Dead skip logic in verify           | `backup-verify.ts`, `backup-create.ts` post-tar cross-check | `excludedPaths` set stored relative paths; `asset.sourcePath` is absolute — `Set.has()` never matched, so excluded assets were always re-checked unnecessarily |
| Uncompressed `.tar` leak on failure | `backup-create.ts` `finally` block                          | `uncompressedTarPath` declared inside `try` block, unreachable from `finally` — leaked the temp file on any error path                                         |
| Hollow `byPattern` in manifest      | `backup-create.ts`                                          | `byPattern` stats only computed when `--json` flag present; manifest always received an empty `[]` otherwise                                                   |

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux 6.8.0 (x64)
- Runtime: Node.js 25.6.1

### Steps

```bash
# 1. Create a state dir with large directories
mkdir -p ~/.openclaw/{venvs,models,logs,completions}
dd if=/dev/zero of=~/.openclaw/venvs/large-file bs=1M count=100

# 2. Backup WITHOUT smart-exclude — includes everything
openclaw backup create --output /tmp/backups --dry-run --json
# → venvs/large-file appears in assets

# 3. Backup WITH smart-exclude — excludes bulky dirs
openclaw backup create --smart-exclude --output /tmp/backups --dry-run --json
# → venvs/ excluded, shown in excludedStats

# 4. Full backup with smart-exclude
openclaw backup create --smart-exclude --output /tmp/backups --verify
# → Completes successfully, verify passes

# 5. Custom exclude pattern
openclaw backup create --exclude "*.log" --exclude "**/__pycache__" --output /tmp/backups

# 6. Protected path guard (exact match)
openclaw backup create --exclude "credentials/" --output /tmp/backups
# → Warning: pattern matches protected path

# 7. Protected path guard (glob match — also caught)
openclaw backup create --exclude "cred*" --output /tmp/backups
# → Warning: pattern "cred*" would match protected path "credentials/"

# 8. Override protected path guard
openclaw backup create --exclude "credentials/" --allow-exclude-protected --output /tmp/backups
```

## Evidence

- [x] Unit tests passing (500 lines, 42 tests)
- [x] Integration tests passing (562 lines, 15 tests)
- [x] CLI forwarding tests passing (159 lines, 10 tests)
- [x] Verify command handles archives with and without `excludedStats`
- [x] Backward compatible with existing archives (missing `excludedStats` field = normal verify)
- [x] 67 backup-related tests passing; full suite pre-existing failures unrelated to this PR

## Human Verification

- Tested with real 11GB `.openclaw` state directory containing `venvs/` (4.2GB), `models/` (3.1GB), `logs/` (2.6GB)
- `--smart-exclude` reduces backup from 11GB bloated archive → focused 380MB archive in ~45 seconds

target.title: Backup: add encrypted snapshot backup flow
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: OpenClaw had local backup export/verify, but no low-friction folder-backed snapshot flow for encrypted backups plus workspace mirroring.
- Why it matters: users need a simple `backup setup/run/status/list/restore` flow for day-to-day protection and full-host recovery without syncing the live state dir.
- What changed: added folder-backed encrypted snapshot storage, workspace backup setup/run/status, restore/list plumbing, config schema/docs, and the follow-up hardening/regression coverage that addressed the P1/P2 review feedback from #41345.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not add provider-specific cloud APIs, device-identity export/import, or any live state-dir sync behavior.

## Change Type (select all)

- `openclaw backup export` is now the primary local archive command; `backup create` stays as a deprecated alias.
- `backup push` stays as a deprecated alias for snapshot creation through `backup run`.
- `reset` and `uninstall` now recommend `openclaw backup export`.
- Backup config now supports `backup.target` and `backup.encryption.key` for folder-backed encrypted snapshots.

Mitigations: target validation rejects unsafe paths, workspace mirroring rejects symlinked trees, restore validates envelope crypto metadata before decrypt, and restore writes now create/copy through safe parent paths to avoid symlink races into protected directories.

## Problem

Feishu card action callbacks include more than just `action.value` (button values). They also carry `form_value`, `input_value`, `name`, `option`, and `options` fields for form submissions, input components, and select menus.

- Simple button clicks (the only previously supported interaction) behave identically
- No changes to any other files
- The structured JSON payload for form submissions is a new code path that only triggers when form fields are present

target.title: fix: copilot token cache ignores profile rotation on rate limit
target.body:
Closes #43658

## Problem

When multiple `github-copilot` auth profiles are configured, profile rotation after a rate limit has no effect. All profiles end up using the same Copilot API token because `resolveCopilotApiToken()` caches to a single shared file (`github-copilot.token.json`) without tracking which GitHub account produced the token. A profile switch passes a different GitHub PAT, but the cache returns the still-valid token from the rate-limited account.

## Solution

Store a SHA-256 fingerprint (first 16 hex chars) of the GitHub PAT alongside the cached Copilot API token. On cache lookup, compare the fingerprint against the current profile's token. A mismatch forces a fresh token exchange with the GitHub Copilot token endpoint.

### Changes

**`src/providers/github-copilot-token.ts`**
- Added `githubTokenFingerprint` field to `CachedCopilotToken` type
- Added `fingerprintGithubToken()` helper (SHA-256, truncated to 16 hex chars)
- Cache hit now requires fingerprint match in addition to expiry check
- New tokens are saved with the fingerprint of the GitHub PAT used

**`src/providers/github-copilot-token.test.ts`**
- Updated existing cache-hit test to include fingerprint
- Added test: cache bypassed when a different GitHub token (different profile) is used
- Added test: legacy cache entries without fingerprint are treated as cache miss

### Backward compatibility

- Legacy cache files without `githubTokenFingerprint` are treated as a cache miss — the token is re-fetched on the next call and saved with a fingerprint. No migration needed.
- The `githubTokenFingerprint` field is optional (`?`) so older versions reading a new cache file won't break.

## Testing

```
✓ derives baseUrl from token
✓ uses cache when token is still valid
✓ fetches and stores token when cache is missing
✓ bypasses cache when a different github token (profile) is used
✓ bypasses cache when cached entry has no fingerprint (legacy)

Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  5 passed (5)
```

All 38 auth-profiles tests also pass.

target.title: feat: cross-gateway sessions_send and sessions_spawn via gateway.peers
target.body:
## Problem

`sessions_send` and `sessions_spawn` only work within a single gateway. In multi-machine setups (agents split across MacBook, iMac, Mac Studio — each running their own OpenClaw gateway), there's no native way for agents to communicate cross-gateway. The current workaround is SSH scripts (`msg-jarvis`), which are fragile and bypass all gateway auth/routing.

## Solution

Add cross-gateway routing to session tools via three complementary mechanisms:

### 1. `gateway.peers` config map (new)

Named peer gateways so agents can target remote gateways by name instead of raw URLs:

```yaml
gateway:
  peers:
    imac:
      url: wss://imac.local:18789
      token: \${env:IMAC_GATEWAY_TOKEN}
    studio:
      url: wss://studio.local:18789
```

Peer tokens support SecretInput (env refs, secret stores, plaintext).

### 2. `sessions_send`: `gateway` / `gatewayUrl` / `gatewayToken` params

```
sessions_send(sessionKey="agent:jarvis:main", message="hello", gateway="imac")
sessions_send(sessionKey="agent:pepper:main", message="hi", gatewayUrl="wss://custom:18789", gatewayToken="tok")
```

When cross-gateway params are present:
- Label resolution happens on the **remote** gateway
- Message is sent via remote `agent` method
- Wait + history fetch all route to remote
- Response includes `remote: true` flag
- Local session resolution, visibility checks, and A2A flow are bypassed (the remote gateway owns those)

### 3. `sessions_spawn`: same params

```
sessions_spawn(task="run tests", agentId="pepper", gateway="studio")
```

Forwards spawn as an agent message to the remote gateway.

### Shared helper: `gateway-peer.ts`

Resolves cross-gateway targeting from tool params:
1. `gateway: "peerName"` → looks up `gateway.peers[peerName]`
2. `gatewayUrl` + optional `gatewayToken` → explicit URL override  
3. Neither → local gateway (returns undefined, no behavior change)

Peer names take precedence when both `gateway` and `gatewayUrl` are provided.

### Design notes

- **Zero breaking changes** — local path is completely unchanged
- **Pattern already exists** — `message`, `cron`, `gateway`, `nodes`, `canvas` tools already accept `gatewayUrl`/`gatewayToken`; this extends the pattern to session tools
- **Security** — peer URLs are validated through existing `resolveGatewayOptions()` which enforces URL format and token resolution rules

## Changes

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `config/zod-schema.ts` | `gateway.peers` record schema |
| `config/types.gateway.ts` | `GatewayPeerConfig` type |
| `config/schema.labels.ts` | Label for `gateway.peers` |
| `config/schema.help.ts` | Help text for `gateway.peers` |
| `agents/tools/gateway-peer.ts` | **New** — shared peer resolution helper |
| `agents/tools/sessions-send-tool.ts` | Cross-gateway fast path + 3 new schema params |
| `agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.ts` | Cross-gateway fast path + 3 new schema params |
| `agents/tools/gateway-peer.test.ts` | **New** — 9 tests for peer resolution |

## Tests

- 9 new peer resolution tests (named peer, missing peer, explicit URL, precedence, error messages)
- 17 existing sessions tests pass
- 32 existing config schema tests pass

Closes #43605

target.title: fix(cli-runner): scope claude-cli queue to live-session owner identity (#91946)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #91946. Replaces the workspace-scoped queue key that fresh `claude-cli` runs share with the same owner identity that `claude-live-session.ts` already uses for its live-session map. Independent OpenClaw sessions sharing one workspace can now run concurrently while resume safety for the same session is unchanged.

## Approach

Per ClawSweeper review on #91946 (the bot recommended keying at the existing live-session ownership boundary):

- New `buildClaudeOwnerKey({agentAccountId, agentId, authProfileId, sessionId, sessionKey})` helper in `src/agents/cli-runner/helpers.ts`.
- `resolveCliRunQueueKey()` now prefers `:owner:<ownerKey>` over `:workspace:<dir>` when no `cliSessionId` is present. Workspace fallback retained for callers that lack owner identity.
- `buildClaudeLiveKey()` in `claude-live-session.ts` refactored to share the same helper so queue identity and live-session identity stay aligned.
- `execute.ts` call site updated to pass owner identity from `context.params` + `context.effectiveAuthProfileId`.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** Fresh `claude-cli` `sessions_spawn` children sharing one OpenClaw workspace serialized through a single `KeyedAsyncQueue` lane keyed by workspace path, so configured `subagents.maxConcurrent` fan-out collapsed to ~12-second cadence regardless of how many children were spawned.

- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw v2026.6.5 install (`/Users/wanglu241/.local/lib/node_modules/openclaw`) on macOS Darwin 25.4.0 (arm64), Node 26.0.0, with `agents.defaults.subagents.maxConcurrent: 12` and a real `claude-cli/Claude-Opus-4.7-hq` provider routed via the bundled Anthropic CLI runtime. The fix shape in this PR was applied as a 1-line `dist` shim (`resolveCliRunQueueKey` returns `:run:<runId>` instead of `:workspace:<dir>`) so the same code path could run end-to-end without a rebuild; the shim is functionally equivalent to this PR's owner-key fallback for the spawn case.

- **Exact steps or command run after the patch:**
  1. Patched `helpers-DmEWhF5F.js:212` in the installed `openclaw` dist to drop the workspace fallback (per-run key fallback). `openclaw gateway restart`. Confirmed `gateway status` running, probe ok.
  2. Issued five rounds of `sessions_spawn` fan-out from one parent OpenClaw session, each child a 5-second `python3` print/sleep/print probe. Rounds: N=2 (PROBE-N2-X/Y), N=3 (PROBE-V1..V3), N=4 (PROBE-W1..W4), N=6 (PROBE-X1..X6), N=8 (PROBE-Y1..Y8). 23 children total.
  3. Captured `cli exec` rows, child start/end timestamps, and tool-call counts from `/tmp/openclaw/openclaw-2026-06-10.log`.
  4. As a counter-baseline reference, the same 4-spawn shape was run before patching with a `cliqueue-probe enqueueCliRun` shim and produced the strict 12-second serial cadence captured in #91946.

- **Evidence:** Pre-patch baseline — workspace lane serializes fresh runs, captured in #91946 [#issuecomment-4670918725](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/91946#issuecomment-4670918725):

  ```
  21:43:49.737  cliqueue-probe  enqueueCliRun key=claude-cli:workspace:/Users/wanglu241/.openclaw/workspace
  21:43:49…     cli exec  ← child A
  21:44:01…     cli exec  ← child B  (~12.3 s after A)
  21:44:13…     cli exec  ← child C  (~12.0 s after B)
  21:44:25…     cli exec  ← child D  (~12.1 s after C)
  ```

  Post-patch fan-out, redacted log slice for the N=8 round (children Y1..Y8 dispatched within a 9-second wall-clock window — pre-patch this would have taken ~96 s):

  ```
  PROBE-Y1 start=23:23:34.123  end=23:23:39.180   tool_uses=1
  PROBE-Y2 start=23:23:35.259  end=23:23:40.294   tool_uses=1
  PROBE-Y3 start=23:23:36.090  end=23:23:41.134   tool_uses=1
  PROBE-Y4 start=23:23:38.505  end=23:23:43.556   tool_uses=1
  PROBE-Y5 start=23:23:38.349  end=23:23:43.399   tool_uses=1
  PROBE-Y6 start=23:23:40.267  end=23:23:45.325   tool_uses=1
  PROBE-Y7 start=23:23:41.326  end=23:23:46.383   tool_uses=1
  PROBE-Y8 start=23:23:43.156  end=23:23:48.209   tool_uses=1
  ```

  Stress matrix across all five rounds (linked in [#issuecomment-4671803091](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/91946#issuecomment-4671803091)):

  | round | N | success | dispatch span | retries |
  |---|---|---|---|---|
  | N=2 | 2 | 2/2 | 2.0 s | 0 |
  | N=3 | 3 | 3/3 | 2.0 s | 0 |
  | N=4 | 4 | 4/4 | — | 1 (unrelated macOS `date '+%3N'` quirk inside the child agent) |
  | N=6 | 6 | 6/6 | 8.2 s | 0 |
  | N=8 | 8 | 8/8 | 9.0 s | 0 |
  | total | 23 | 23/23 | — | 0 cli backend retries |

- **Observed result after the fix:** Independent fresh `claude-cli` `sessions_spawn` children in one workspace now dispatch their `cli exec` rows within ~1 s of each other instead of every ~12 s, and 23/23 children completed cleanly across 5 fan-out rounds. Same-OpenClaw-session resumed runs continue to serialize per `cliSessionId` (unchanged path), and `buildClaudeLiveKey()` now derives from the same helper so the queue key cannot drift from the live-session lifecycle key.

- **What was not tested:**
  - Multi-workspace concurrent fan-out (the bug only manifested when sharing one workspace; cross-workspace already had distinct keys, no regression risk for that path).
  - Codex / OpenCode CLI backends (`isClaudeCliProvider` short-circuits this code path; their queue key is unchanged).
  - Resume-after-crash race against an in-flight fresh run for the same OpenClaw session (this PR keeps `cliSessionId` precedence over `ownerKey`, so the existing per-session serialization for resume holds; the precedence is exercised by the new "prefers cliSessionId over ownerKey" unit test).

## Open questions for maintainer

1. Should the workspace fallback be kept (current PR) or removed? Removing tightens the contract but breaks any caller that doesn't have owner identity yet.
2. `buildClaudeOwnerKey` currently uses the same JSON.stringify field order as `buildClaudeLiveKey`'s previous inline expression. If you'd prefer canonical key sorting, happy to switch.
3. Is there a reason `execute.ts` shouldn't have access to `context.effectiveAuthProfileId` here? It's read for the live-session path already, so this PR assumes the same exposure is fine for the queue key.

target.title: fix(agent): dampen Discord stale thread replies
target.body:
## Summary
- add Discord-specific message-tool-only guidance only for Discord group/channel turns
- thread canonical chat type through queued, embedded, and compaction prompt paths
- cover group/channel positives, direct-message negatives, queued run creation, and runner forwarding

## Diagnosis
This was not a Twilio delivery bug. In a Discord thread where final assistant text is private, the runtime correctly exposed `message(action=send)` as the visible reply path. The prompt guidance needed to make the channel-noise tradeoff explicit, but only for Discord group/channel contexts. Direct messages should not receive stale-thread quiet-etiquette guidance.

The follow-up `check-test-types` CI failure was related: the new queued-run assertion exposed that `FollowupRun.run` did not type or forward the normalized `chatType` through the normal reply runner path.

## Tests
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-run.media-only.test.ts`

target.title: feat(cron): on-exit schedule — wake on a watched command's exit
target.body:
## Summary

Adds an **`on-exit` cron schedule kind**: a job that fires once when a watched shell command exits, starting a fresh agent turn in the originating conversation. It's "a #83738 cron wake, triggered by a process exit instead of by the tool call" — it reuses the merged origin-aware wake as its delivery mechanism and only adds the *trigger*.

- **The gap.** openclaw already wakes the parent when an *openclaw-managed* child finishes — sub-agents, isolated cron runs, and image/video/music generation raise `task_completion` internal events consumed by the embedded-agent runtime. But nothing watches an **arbitrary external command**, and the CLI backends can't participate in that internal system at all: a command a `claude-cli` agent runs via its exec tool lives inside the per-turn process tree that's `signalProcessTree(SIGTERM→SIGKILL)`'d at turn end (#71662), so it dies before it can observe an exit or fire a wake. The only pre-existing workaround is a polling `every:Nm` cron that burns a turn per poll.
- **What this does.** A new `CronSchedule` kind `{ kind: "on-exit", command, cwd? }`, executed by a **gateway-ProcessSupervisor-owned watcher** (`cron-exit-watchers.ts`). The watcher runs the command as a child of the *gateway* supervisor — outside any agent turn's process tree — so it survives turn teardown by construction. On exit it fires #83738's origin-aware wake on the job's captured session, so the woken turn **continues the originating conversation** (native `claude-cli --resume`, see proof), with the exit code folded into the woken turn's text. One-shot per arm.
- **Surfaces.** `on-exit` is accepted by the protocol schema, the cron tool (`schedule.kind:"on-exit"` + `command`/`cwd`), and the CLI (`cron add --on-exit <shell> [--on-exit-cwd <path>]`). Watchers arm on job add/update and on gateway-startup reconcile (so persisted jobs re-arm after a restart) and cancel on remove/disable/shutdown.
- **AI-assisted:** yes — drafted with Claude.

## Why a *non*-internally-managed wake (and why it works for every backend)

The internal `task_completion` machinery is embedded-runtime-bound and command-agnostic — it has no "watch a shell command" task type, for any backend. A `claude-cli` (or `claude-cli-interactive`/proxy) agent can't register an external command with it, and a command it runs inline dies at turn teardown. So the watcher must live outside the turn and outside the embedded-agent event system — the gateway ProcessSupervisor. Because the wake then rides the gateway **command lane** (busy-detection is `getQueueSize(Main)`, acquired at the dispatch layer *above* the backend), it behaves identically on plain `claude-cli` and the interactive MITM backend: if the watched command exits mid-turn, the wake hits `requests-in-flight`, re-queues, and fires when the lane frees — never concurrently. Proven below on plain `claude-cli`; the interactive path is the same lane mechanism.

## Real behavior proof

Real OpenClaw gateway (built from this branch) + real `claude-cli` (claude-sonnet-4-6) backend with native session resume (`resumeArgs`), in a Linux container. A persisted `on-exit` job (`command: "sleep 22"`) is loaded at gateway startup; a binding turn establishes the session; the watched command exits; the watcher fires the origin wake. Gateway logs ([gw-clean.log](https://github.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/blob/proof-assets-onexit/gw-clean.log), [cron-exit-structured.log](https://github.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/blob/proof-assets-onexit/cron-exit-structured.log)):

```
20:45:02  cron-exit: watcher armed                          jobId=onexit-hl command="sleep 22"   <- armed from the PERSISTED job at startup (survives restart)
20:45:12  cli exec  trigger=heartbeat  useResume=false      <- binding turn establishes the cli session
20:45:17  (binding turn finished)
20:45:27  cron-exit: watched command exited; firing job     exitCode=0 reason=exit                <- watched command exits -> watcher fires the origin wake
20:45:46  cli exec  trigger=heartbeat  useResume=true  resumeSession=1b22992cb50d  reuse=reusable  historyPrompt=present
                                                             ^- the woken turn NATIVELY --resumes the originating session (not history-replay)
```

The wake turn ran *after* the binding turn finished, so it is unambiguously the on-exit wake — and it natively `--resume`d the binding turn's claude-cli session, continuing the conversation. A separate run also exercised the **mid-turn case**: the watched command exited while a turn was in flight, the wake hit `requests-in-flight`, re-queued, and the woken turn ran after the in-flight turn ended (never concurrently).

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** an agent (any backend, esp. `claude-cli`) cannot reliably arm "wake me when this external command exits" — managed `task_completion` doesn't cover external commands and an agent-spawned command dies at turn teardown.
- **Real environment tested:** real gateway from this branch + real `claude-cli` backend with `resumeArgs`, Linux container; on-exit job persisted to the store and armed at startup.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** seed a persisted `on-exit` job (`sleep 22`, sessionKey bound), start the gateway, run a binding turn, let the command exit.
- **Evidence after fix:** redacted gateway logs above ([gw-clean.log](https://github.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/blob/proof-assets-onexit/gw-clean.log), [cron-exit-structured.log](https://github.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/blob/proof-assets-onexit/cron-exit-structured.log)) showing arm → exit → fire → native-resume woken turn.
- **Observed result after fix:** the watcher armed from the persisted job, the watched command's exit fired the origin wake, and the woken turn natively `--resume`d the originating session.
- **What was not tested:** the Telegram-thread visual (the session is a local key here; thread delivery is #83738's merged + proven mechanism — the wake routing is identical regardless of channel). The interactive/MITM backend (same command-lane mechanism; not separately captured).

### Bug caught by the live proof
`getInvalidPersistedCronJobReason` only allowed `at/every/cron`, so on-exit jobs **failed persistence validation** — they couldn't be saved to the store or survive a restart (the watcher arms from the store at startup). Fixed + regression test. This is exactly the class of issue unit tests around the schema/codec missed and the live store-seed proof surfaced.

## Limitations & maintainer notes

- **Command execution policy.** The watched command runs under the gateway ProcessSupervisor with a 24h safety timeout and the `OPENCLAW_CLI` env marker — consistent with how cron `command`-payload jobs run (`runCommandWithTimeout`). It does **not** apply the full `tools.exec` policy (host approvals, sandbox selection, unsafe-command guards, script preflight); neither do cron command-payload jobs. Unifying an approvals/sandbox policy across *both* gateway-level command-execution paths is a cross-cutting product/security decision flagged for maintainer ownership, not narrowed here.
- **Downgrade.** `on-exit` is a new schedule kind. A rollback to a release predating it will not decode these rows (older code filters unknown schedule kinds on load), and a later cron write can drop them — inherent to introducing any new schedule kind. Treat on-exit jobs as not downgrade-preserved until a versioned-store strategy lands.

## Tests / CI

- New: `cron-exit-watchers.test.ts` (arm/fire-on-exit/skip-timer-and-disabled/idempotent/cancel/no-fire-after-cancel/one-shot), `persisted-shape.onexit.test.ts`, `schedule-options.onexit.test.ts` (CLI), `row-codec.schedule.test.ts` (store round-trip), plus on-exit cases in schedule/normalize.
- tsgo clean; full cron suite + watcher/CLI suites green; full Linux unit suite run on this head (results in the re-review comment).

## Summary

- Fixes custom Anthropic-compatible providers whose selected model API is `anthropic-messages` but whose provider id is not literally `anthropic`.
- Carries the selected catalog/config model API into thinking policy resolution so Anthropic Messages routes can expose `adaptive`/`max` levels instead of falling back to generic/off behavior.
- Keeps owner precedence intact: direct bundled provider policy still wins, exact runtime provider hooks can override API-family fallback, and API-family bundled policy only runs after provider-owned hooks decline.
- Out of scope: changing provider auth, model catalog contents, config shape, migrations, or transport behavior.

## Linked context

Closes #91975

Related: the issue's ClawSweeper review identified the missing API fact in thinking-policy dispatch and recommended reusing API-owner routing without provider-id substring matching.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Behavior addressed: Custom providers with `api: "anthropic-messages"` now route thinking profile lookup through the Anthropic policy fallback after exact provider hooks decline, so Claude models expose Anthropic thinking levels instead of silently losing extended thinking controls.
Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw CLI/source checkout on Node 24, using the PR branch at commit `3b413143dbc6f4a64149e00106403aa651e6933a` with dependencies installed via `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm openclaw --version`; then `node --import tsx --input-type=module` with OpenClaw's `resolveProviderThinkingProfile` runtime resolver for provider `jdcloud-anthropic`, model `claude-sonnet-4-6`, and API `anthropic-messages`.
Evidence after fix: Copied console output from the local OpenClaw source checkout:

```text
$ pnpm openclaw --version
OpenClaw 2026.6.2 (3b41314)

$ node --import tsx --input-type=module <<'END_PROOF_SCRIPT'
import { resolveProviderThinkingProfile } from './src/plugins/provider-thinking.ts';

const provider = 'jdcloud-anthropic';
const modelId = 'claude-sonnet-4-6';
const api = 'anthropic-messages';
const profile = resolveProviderThinkingProfile({
  provider,
  context: {
    provider,
    api,
    modelId,
    params: {},
    reasoning: undefined,
    compat: undefined,
  },
});

console.log(`openclaw_source_commit=${process.env.OPENCLAW_PROOF_COMMIT ?? '3b413143dbc6f4a64149e00106403aa651e6933a'}`);
console.log(`provider=${provider}`);
console.log(`model=${modelId}`);
console.log(`api=${api}`);
console.log(`thinkingProfile=${profile ? 'resolved' : 'missing'}`);
console.log(`thinkingLevels=${profile?.levels.map((level) => level.id).join(',') ?? 'none'}`);
console.log(`thinkingDefault=${profile?.defaultLevel ?? 'none'}`);
END_PROOF_SCRIPT
openclaw_source_commit=3b413143dbc6f4a64149e00106403aa651e6933a
provider=jdcloud-anthropic
model=claude-sonnet-4-6
api=anthropic-messages
thinkingProfile=resolved
thinkingLevels=off,minimal,low,medium,high,adaptive,max
thinkingDefault=adaptive
```

Observed result after fix: The real OpenClaw resolver returns a resolved Anthropic thinking profile for the custom `jdcloud-anthropic` provider because the selected model API is `anthropic-messages`; the exposed levels include `adaptive` and `max`, and the default is `adaptive`.
What was not tested: No live external Anthropic/JDCloud API request was made; this change is local provider thinking-policy/profile resolution, not network transport or auth.
Proof limitations or environment constraints: The proof uses the local OpenClaw source runtime resolver and CLI version output rather than a remote provider account; focused tests, lint, build, and autoreview are listed below as supplemental validation.
Before evidence (optional but encouraged): The regression tests added in this PR encode the prior failure mode where the selected model API was available in catalog/config but was not passed into provider thinking-policy dispatch.

## Tests and validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/thinking.test.ts src/plugins/provider-runtime.test.ts src/plugins/provider-public-artifacts.test.ts extensions/anthropic/provider-policy-api.test.ts`

- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm build`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local`

Regression coverage added for API propagation, bundled policy lookup by manifest model-catalog API refs, Anthropic Messages custom-provider profiles, model-level API override precedence, runtime hook precedence over API fallback, and runtime fallback when an unrelated API-ref bundled surface declines.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? Yes

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? No

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? No

Highest-risk area: Provider thinking-policy ownership and fallback ordering.

Risk mitigation: Direct bundled policy remains first for literal bundled providers, runtime provider hooks win before API-family fallback, and common/non-unique API refs that decline no longer block runtime provider hooks.

target.title: fix(cron): skip deleteAfterRun and preserve counters for manual runs (#83538)
target.body:
## Summary

`openclaw cron run` (manual/debug runs) on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes the job on success — consuming the one-shot and preventing its scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate `consecutiveErrors`/`consecutiveSkipped` counters, interfering with scheduled retry/backoff state.

This PR adds an `isManual` flag to `applyJobResult` so manual runs preserve the job and leave scheduled execution state untouched.

## Root cause

`applyJobResult()` has no concept of manual vs scheduled runs. When a manual run succeeds on a `deleteAfterRun: true` at-job, `shouldDelete` evaluates to `true` and the job is removed from the store — the scheduled fire never happens.

Additionally, manual error/skipped/success outcomes unconditionally mutate `consecutiveErrors` and `consecutiveSkipped` counters, which control retry backoff and auto-disable behavior for scheduled runs.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `timer.ts` | Add `isManual` option to `applyJobResult`; skip `deleteAfterRun` for manual runs; guard all counter mutations and alert emissions with `!isManual` |
| `ops.ts` | Pass `isManual: true` from manual run completion and invalid-persisted-run handling |
| `timer.regression.test.ts` | 5 new tests: manual ok/error preserve job, manual doesn't increment/reset counters |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts
 ✓ cron (45 tests) 144ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  45 passed (45)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes it, preventing scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate scheduled retry counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at `aa92a4a5b0`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0.

### Source-level proof

```
Main branch applyJobResult():
  - shouldDelete = kind === "at" && deleteAfterRun && status === "ok"
    → no manual discriminator, always deletes on success
  - consecutiveErrors incremented unconditionally on error
  - consecutiveSkipped incremented unconditionally on skip

Fix branch:
  - shouldDelete = wouldDelete && !opts?.isManual
    → manual runs never trigger deletion
  - Counter mutations guarded: if (!opts?.isManual) { ... }
    → manual runs don't affect retry/backoff state
```

### Regression test coverage

```
✅ manual ok on deleteAfterRun at-job → job preserved, not deleted
✅ manual error on deleteAfterRun at-job → job preserved, not deleted  
✅ scheduled ok on deleteAfterRun at-job → job deleted (unchanged behavior)
✅ manual error does not increment consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 → still 3)
✅ manual success does not reset consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 → still 3)
```

- **What was not tested**: Live `openclaw cron run` CLI flow (requires a running gateway with a configured `deleteAfterRun` at-job). The `isManual` flag propagation from `ops.ts` to `applyJobResult` is validated at the unit level.

Closes #83538


### Re-verification (2026-05-20)

Local checkout at `d89f14de26`, macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`.

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts

 ✓ cron  ../../src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts (39 tests) 116ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  39 passed (39)
   Duration  152ms
```

Full cron suite (`src/cron/`) also run: 94 test files, 901 passed. The 7 failures are in unrelated files (`run-log.test.ts`, `service.failure-alert.test.ts`) pre-existing on upstream/main.


## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: `openclaw cron run` (manual) of a `deleteAfterRun: true` at-job incorrectly deletes the job. Manual runs also pollute `consecutiveErrors`/`consecutiveSkipped` counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched checkout at `0cec2acc7a`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On `upstream/main`, counter mutations and `shouldDelete` run unconditionally — no `isManual` discriminator exists.
- **Exact steps after patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts` + `src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts`
- **Evidence after fix**: 46/46 timer regression tests pass (including 7 new #83538/#83933-specific tests). 7/7 failure-alert tests pass.
- **What was not tested**: Live Gateway CLI flow; the tests exercise the same `applyJobResult` code path the Gateway calls internally.

### Key test results

| Scenario | Result |
|----------|--------|
| Manual run + deleteAfterRun + success | NOT deleted ✅ |
| Scheduled run + deleteAfterRun + success | Deleted ✅ |
| Manual error + consecutiveErrors=3 | Stays 3 ✅ |
| Manual success + consecutiveErrors=5 | Stays 5 ✅ |
| Manual error → failure alert | NOT triggered ✅ |
| Scheduled error → failure alert | Triggered ✅ |


## Real behavior proof (Docker, 2026-05-21)

**Environment**: Docker container, `node:24-bookworm-slim` (aarch64), patched source at `885d605e56`.

### Latest fixes

| Issue | Problem | Fix |
|-------|---------|-----|
| P1 | Manual at-job error was disabling job (`enabled=false`, `nextRunAtMs=undefined`) | At-job error retry/disable block now guarded with `if (opts?.isManual)` |
| P2 | Manual success was clearing `lastFailureAlertAtMs` cooldown | Cooldown clearing now inside `if (!opts?.isManual)` guard |

### Test results

| Test Suite | Result |
|------------|--------|
| Timer regression (49 tests) | ✅ All passed |
| Failure-alert (7 tests) | ✅ All passed |

### Key verified behaviors

| Scenario | Result |

### Manual-run state contract

`cron run` is a debug/verification command. Manual runs record their outcome (lastRunAtMs, lastStatus, lastError) but do not advance scheduled state. This makes manual runs safe, non-destructive operations that cannot interfere with scheduled execution. No reasonable user scenario depends on manual runs consuming one-shot jobs or driving retry/backoff state.

## Real behavior proof (Docker, HEAD `8596607fca`, 2026-06-02 — backoff fix)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` on a recurring job that errors must not open a backoff window that blocks the next scheduled fire. Previously, `isErrorBackoffPending()`, `resolveJobErrorBackoffUntilMs()`, `isPendingErrorBackoffSlot()`, and `recomputeJobNextRunAtMs()` read `lastRunStatus` and `lastRunAtMs` without checking `lastRunWasManual`, causing a manual error to push `nextRunAtMs` forward and block the timer-scheduled fire.

- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main, `resolveJobErrorBackoffUntilMs()` does not check `lastRunWasManual`. target.title: fix(agents): do not misclassify client-disconnect abort as run timeout
target.body:
### Summary

- **Problem**: Issue #90764 reports that embedded agent runs abort at ~165–180 s wall-clock regardless of `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds` or `models.providers.*.timeoutSeconds`. The run exits with "Request timed out before a response was generated." and the message advises increasing `timeoutSeconds`, but increasing it has no effect on the abort timing.
- **Root Cause**: `onAbort` and `onExternalAbortSignal` in `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts` call `isTimeoutError(reason)` on the abort-signal reason to decide whether to set `timedOut = true`. `isTimeoutError` calls `hasTimeoutHint`, which calls `isTimeoutErrorMessage` — that function matches the pattern `/\boperation was aborted\b/i` against the error message. `AbortController.abort()` with no arguments produces `DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError")`; that message matches the pattern, so `hasTimeoutHint` returns `true` and `isTimeoutError` returns `true` for what is actually a plain client cancellation, setting `timedOut = true` erroneously. `watchClientDisconnect` in `src/gateway/http-common.ts` calls `abortController.abort()` with no args whenever the HTTP socket closes — for example, when a reverse-proxy or keepalive layer evicts the connection after a long period of no SSE output during a thinking phase. This fires `params.abortSignal`, which triggers `onAbort`, which then sets `timedOut = true` with no relation to the configured timeout values.
- **Fix**: Replace `isTimeoutError` with a new `isSignalTimeoutReason` export from `src/agents/failover-error.ts`. `isSignalTimeoutReason` checks only `readErrorName(reason) === "TimeoutError"` — the name produced exclusively by `AbortSignal.timeout()` and `makeTimeoutAbortReason()`, the two intentional timeout-creation paths. Plain `AbortError` (`name = "AbortError"`) is a cancellation and returns `false`. `isTimeoutError` is unchanged for all other callers that classify general LLM error messages, where the Ollama NDJSON / stream-abort patterns still apply.
- **What changed**:
  - `src/agents/failover-error.ts` — export `isSignalTimeoutReason`: checks only `name === "TimeoutError"`, no text-matching heuristic.
  - `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts` — replace `isTimeoutError` import and both abort-signal handler call sites (`onExternalAbortSignal` and `onAbort`) with `isSignalTimeoutReason`.
  - `src/agents/failover-error.test.ts` — add `isSignalTimeoutReason` describe block: plain `AbortError` DOMException, ABORT_TIMEOUT_RE `AbortError`, `AbortSignal.timeout()` DOMException, `makeTimeoutAbortReason()`-style Error, null/undefined.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**:
  - `isTimeoutError` and its text-matching heuristics are unchanged; the change is scoped to the two abort-signal handler call sites.
  - Config surface unchanged (no schema, defaults, doctor migrations, or `docs/reference/config`).
  - Plugin surface unchanged (no plugin SDK, manifest, `extensions/*` api/runtime-api, registry/loader).
  - Gateway protocol unchanged.

### Reproduction

1. Configure `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds: 600` and `models.providers.custom-api-anthropic-com.timeoutSeconds: 600`.
2. Start a run that completes several tool calls, then enters a thinking phase that produces no SSE output for long enough to trigger the client's HTTP connection idle timeout.
3. The reverse proxy or keepalive layer evicts the idle connection; `watchClientDisconnect` fires `abortController.abort()` with no args.
4. **Before this PR**: `onAbort` calls `isTimeoutError(DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError"))` — `hasTimeoutHint` matches the message via `isTimeoutErrorMessage` (`/\boperation was aborted\b/i`), returns `true`; `timedOut = true`; run exits at ~165–180 s with "Request timed out before a response was generated."; increasing `timeoutSeconds` has no effect.
5. **After this PR**: `isSignalTimeoutReason(DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError"))` checks `readErrorName(reason) === "TimeoutError"` → `false`; `timedOut` is not set; the abort is treated as an external cancellation, not a run timeout.

### Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed (#90764)**: after the fix, a client-disconnect abort no longer sets `timedOut = true` in the embedded attempt; only abort-signal reasons with `name === "TimeoutError"` (from `AbortSignal.timeout()` or `makeTimeoutAbortReason()`) are treated as run timeouts.

**Real environment tested (Linux, Node 22 — Vitest against the production `isSignalTimeoutReason` and the two production abort-signal handler paths)**: `isSignalTimeoutReason` driven directly with each discriminating input; `onAbort` / `onExternalAbortSignal` call sites exercised via the existing full-runner `attempt.test.ts` suite.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `pnpm test src/agents/failover-error.test.ts`; `pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.test.ts`; `pnpm format:check` and `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs` on the three changed files; `pnpm tsgo`.

**Evidence after fix**:

```
failover-error.test.ts   Tests  88 passed (88)
attempt.test.ts          Tests  121 passed (121)
```

The `isSignalTimeoutReason` describe covers: `DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError")` → `false` (watchClientDisconnect regression case; old `isTimeoutError` returned `true` via `isTimeoutErrorMessage` matching `/\boperation was aborted\b/i`); `Error("request aborted", name="AbortError")` → `false` (old `isTimeoutError` returned `true` via `ABORT_TIMEOUT_RE`); `DOMException("signal timed out", "TimeoutError")` → `true`; `Error("request timed out", name="TimeoutError")` → `true`; null/undefined → `false`.

**Observed result after fix**: `isSignalTimeoutReason(new DOMException("This operation was aborted", "AbortError"))` returns `false`; the abort-signal handlers classify client disconnections as `externalAbort=true / timedOut=false`; the run does not surface "Request timed out" for network-layer connection evictions.

**What was not tested**: live gateway repro with a real reverse-proxy idle eviction end-to-end; the abort-signal handler logic and the `isSignalTimeoutReason` discriminator are covered deterministically against the production functions, but a full network-layer round-trip was not driven.

### Risk / Mitigation

- **Risk**: `isSignalTimeoutReason` is strictly narrower than `isTimeoutError` for the two affected call sites; a timeout path that passes `params.abortSignal` with an error whose `name` is not `"TimeoutError"` will no longer be classified as a timeout.
- **Mitigation**: both intentional internal timeout-creation paths (`makeTimeoutAbortReason()` and `AbortSignal.timeout()`) produce `name === "TimeoutError"`; `isTimeoutError` is unchanged for all LLM error classification callers where the broader text-matching heuristic is appropriate.

### Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

### Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Agent runtime
- [x] Tests

### Linked Issue/PR

Fixes #90764

target.title: fix(feishu): use union_id as fallback for @mention detection in multi-bot groups
target.body:
## Problem

Fixes #40768

When multiple Feishu bots share the same group, @mentioning a specific bot is not recognized. The bot logs `message in group ... did not mention bot` even though it's correctly mentioned.

## Root Cause

Feishu `open_id` is **app-scoped** — the same bot has a different `open_id` depending on which app's token is used to query it. Confirmed by querying the same message with two different bot tokens:

```
# Same @mention, different app contexts:
writer token → mention open_id: ou_4ede388c79638a9057a432c3e3832f91
tron token   → mention open_id: ou_04dc13327d491d9e36da57826a4131f0
```

WebSocket push events deliver mention `open_id`s in a specific app context that may not match the receiving bot's own `open_id`, causing `checkBotMentioned()` to always return `false`.

## Fix

`union_id` is globally unique across all apps for the same entity — it doesn't vary by app context. This PR:

1. Fetches `union_id` alongside `open_id` from `bot/v3/info` at startup
2. Stores it in a new `botUnionIds` map in monitor state
3. Passes it through to `checkBotMentioned()` as a fallback
4. Matches mentions by `union_id` when `open_id` doesn't match

The `open_id` match is preserved as the primary check; `union_id` is a fallback, so existing behavior is unchanged for single-bot setups.

## Files Changed

- `types.ts`: add `botUnionId` to `FeishuProbeResult`
- `probe.ts`: extract `union_id` from `bot/v3/info` response
- `monitor.startup.ts`: propagate `botUnionId` through `FeishuMonitorBotIdentity`
- `monitor.state.ts`: add `botUnionIds` map with proper clear/delete
- `monitor.account.ts`: store/clear `botUnionId`, pass to `handleFeishuMessage`
- `bot.ts`: accept `botUnionId` in `checkBotMentioned` and `parseFeishuMessageEvent`, match by `union_id` when `open_id` mismatches
